Off Calendar Aug-10-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Alexander P. Grayner of the law offices of Buchalter (415-227-3546) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Randel J. Campbell of the law offices of Lynch Gilardi & Grummer (415-273-2355) appears by telephone as counsel for defendant/demurring party, Pankow Special Projects LP

Attorney, Evan M. Reese of the law offices of Collins Collins Muir + Stewart LLP (510-844-5100) appears as counsel for co-defendant/demurringing party, Royston, Hanamoto, Alley & Abey

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT PANKOW SPECIAL PROJECTS, LP'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT is OVERRULED. Liberally construed, the complaint's factual allegations do not show that the defects were obvious ones discoverable upon a reasonable inspection, nor was plaintiff required to hire an expert to investigate for construction defects upon the occurrence of minor maintenance problems. (Creekridge Townhome Owners Assn., Inc. v. C. Scott Whitten, Inc. (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 251, 256-59; Geertz v. Ausonio (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 1363, 1371.) Plaintiff has sufficiently pled the time and manner of the discovery of the alleged latent defects (FAC 15, 17-20) and the reason for the late discovery has also been alleged (id. at 13-15). Thus, on its face, the complaint is not time-barred. While claims for implied warranties against professionals such a defendants are not proper, claims for express warranties based on a written or oral contract can be maintained. (Gagne v. Bertran (1954) 43 Cal.2d 481, 487.) Plaintiff has sufficiently alleged an express warranty based on a written contract. (FAC 37.) The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-10-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Alexander P. Grayner of the law offices of Buchalter (415-227-3546) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Evan M. Reese of the law offices of Collins Collins Muir + Stewart LLP (510-844-5100) appears as counsel for defendant/demurringing party, Royston, Hanamoto, Alley & Abey

Attorney, Randel J. Campbell of the law offices of Lynch Gilardi & Grummer (415-273-2355) appears by telephone as counsel for co-defendant/demurring party, Pankow Special Projects LP

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANTS ROYSTON, HANAMOTO, ALLEY & ABEY'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT is OVERRULED as to the first and third causes of action for breach of contract and negligence and SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND as to the second cause of action for breach of warranty.

Liberally construed, the complaint's factual allegations do not show that the defects were obvious ones discoverable upon a reasonable inspection, nor was plaintiff required to hire an expert to investigate for construction defects upon the occurrence of minor maintenance problems. (See Creekridge Townhome Owners Assn., Inc. v. C. Scott Whitten, Inc. (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 251, 256-59; Geertz v. Ausonio (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 1363, 1371.) Plaintiff has sufficiently pled the time and manner of discovery of the alleged latent defects (FAC 15, 17-20) and the reason for the late discovery has also been alleged (id. at 13-15). Thus, on its face, the complaint is not time-barred. While claims for implied warranties against design professionals are not proper, claims for express warranties based on a written or oral contract can be maintained. (Gagne v. Bertran (1954) 43 Cal.2d 481, 487.) The written contract language relied upon against RHAA cannot be fairly read to provide an express warranty, because the promises that RHAA would visit the site and reject non-conforming work does not guarantee a particular result. Plaintiff is given leave to sufficiently allege the oral contract on which its express warranty claim is based. (See Stevenson v. San Francisco Housing Authority (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 269, 284.). The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.
